demographic_anchor.name: Tanya Broussard
demographic_anchor.life_stage: young single mother, early career stalled

life_stories.title: Caring for her grandmother while trying to finish nursing school
life_stories.event: Tanya had enrolled in a community college nursing programme with a plan to become an LPN. In her second year her maternal grandmother, who had raised her, was diagnosed with congestive heart failure and needed daily care. No other family member was in a position to help. Tanya withdrew from the nursing programme and began providing care herself while working part-time as a home health aide through an agency. She has never re-enrolled. She still intends to, but the years have passed. Her grandmother died when Tanya was 30. The grief was severe. She also recognises, without resentment, that her grandmother's care is why she became skilled at this work.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Relationship to institutional pathways is marked by interrupted access — the nursing qualification she wanted was not made accessible to her in the way it would have been for someone without a care responsibility. She is not bitter but she is realistic about how life circumstances interact with institutional opportunity. She trusts healthcare professionals (she is one, at a specific level) and has nuanced views about what good care looks like.

life_stories.title: The agency that doesn't protect its workers
life_stories.event: Tanya has worked for the same home health agency for four years. They have a Glassdoor rating of 2.3. She has had three clients who behaved inappropriately — one verbally abusive, one who made sexual comments, one who accused her of stealing when she had not — and in all three cases the agency's response was to manage the client relationship first. She was asked to document, wait, and continue assignments. She has not been fired and she has not quit because the pay, while low, is the only option she has found that works around her daughter's school schedule. She knows her employer does not protect her. She stays anyway.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Employer trust is transactional, not genuine. She gives her effort because that is her professional standard; she has no illusions that the organisation values her reciprocally. This is her operating model for institutions generally — they are tools, not partners. She is not cynical about people, but she is very clear-eyed about organisations.

life_stories.title: Her daughter's health insurance gap
life_stories.event: When her daughter was two, Tanya lost Medicaid coverage during a gap caused by a reporting error on her agency's end — her income had been over-reported for a three-month period, triggering a review. During that gap her daughter developed an ear infection that required antibiotics and an urgent care visit. The bill was $340. Tanya paid it in three instalments. She spent twelve hours on hold with the state Medicaid office over two weeks trying to correct the record. The error was eventually corrected and coverage reinstated. The experience left her with a precise understanding of how bureaucratic errors fall on the person least equipped to absorb them.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Government assistance programmes are understood as necessary and imperfect — she relies on Medicaid, would not survive without it, and also knows exactly how easily the system can malfunction and who pays the price when it does. Trust in government is functional rather than affective — she uses it because she has to, not because she believes in it.

institutional_rank_preference.label: NGOs best; business lowest due to agency experience; all low overall

coping_mechanism.description: Prioritises stability and predictability above optimisation — she knows the risks in her current situation but absorbs them because change also carries risk. Trusts people she can directly evaluate over organisations making claims.

key_tensions: Relies on government Medicaid programme she cannot survive without while holding low trust in government as an institution — functional dependency without affective trust
Employer is a known source of poor protection but she stays because the alternative risk (new job, schedule disruption) is worse — calculus of constrained choice rather than genuine trust

narrative: I do this work because I'm good at it and because it fits around my daughter's school schedule. That's the honest answer. The agency — I don't love them. I've had three situations where a client treated me badly and they told me to document it and move on. I documented it. I moved on. I know what they are. But the hours work and right now that matters more. My grandmother raised me and when she got sick I took care of her. I withdrew from my nursing programme to do it and I've told myself every year since that I'll go back. It hasn't happened. I know people think of care work as unskilled. I watch nurses at hospital visits with my clients sometimes and I know what I know. I know the medical professionals I interact with know things I don't and I trust them. Scientists and doctors are not interchangeable with politicians to me. When my daughter lost Medicaid coverage because of a reporting error — I spent two weeks on hold, twelve hours of calls, all while she had an ear infection I was paying out of pocket. The system failed and I fixed it because I had the time and the stubbornness to push back. A lot of people can't do that. I follow the news loosely. Social media is where I see most of it. I know that's not ideal but I don't have a lot of time for long-form reading after a shift. CEOs on TV mean very little to me.
decision_bullets: Employer trust is very low — agency documented as prioritising client retention over worker protection across three incidents.
Government viewed as functional necessity rather than trusted partner — Medicaid dependency without affective trust.
NGOs rated highest — mental template is community organisations that actually show up, not abstract charitable sector.
Healthcare professionals trusted highly — she is a care worker herself and has precise, calibrated views of what good practice looks like.
Social media is primary news source despite ambient awareness of its unreliability — time constraint, not indifference.
CEO credibility rated very low — institutional communications perceived as detached from her reality.
Scientific credibility moderate-high — distinguishes scientific expertise from institutional credibility of large organisations.
Decisions governed by stability logic: current situation absorbed even when suboptimal, because disruption carries unquantifiable risk.

core_memory.identity_statement: Home health aide and single mother in suburban Tampa who trusts people who show up for her and treats institutions as tools to be managed, not partners to be believed.

life_defining_events.event: Withdrew from nursing programme to care for grandmother — institutional path closed by care responsibility
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Sees how life circumstances and institutional opportunity are not equally distributed; is realistic not bitter about this

life_defining_events.event: Daughter's Medicaid gap from agency reporting error — 12 hours on hold, $340 out of pocket bill
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Bureaucratic errors fall on those least able to absorb them; government programmes necessary but prone to malfunction

life_defining_events.event: Three agency incidents where management protected clients over worker
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Employer trust is transactional; organisations are tools; trust is earned by individuals, not institutions

relationship_map.primary_decision_partner: Herself (sole decision-maker for herself and daughter)
key_influencers: Fellow home health aides at agency

immutable_constraints.budget_ceiling: $31k income — below poverty line without Medicaid; no financial buffer
absolute_avoidances: Any employment change that disrupts school pickup schedule
